trend_name: The Triumphant Return of the French Twist
description:
Every product on this page was chosen by a Harper's BAZAAR editor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
The Old Hollywood hair trend is making a very modern comeback
Every season, the Row does something that leaves the fashion world in a frenzy, whether it’s the latest incarnation of the cult-favorite Margaux bag, a high-vamp jelly sandal in lollipop red, or, as was the case for spring/summer 2026, a hair comb (or several) tucked ever so neatly into a sculptural, modern take on a French twist.
It was the updo seen around the world, with photos quickly coursing through Instagram andTikTok, prompting an uptick in French-twist tutorials. The runway look was created by legendary hairstylistGuido Palau, who credits its virality to its familiarity. “When there’s a hairstyle that has one foot in the past and one foot in the future, people respond well to it,” he explains. “The combs could trigger a memory, maybe from a grandparent wearing hair combs in her hair or from a painting.” It’s a style that is also accessible. “There’s something nostalgic, but it feels feminine, sculptural, and modern all at the same time—and something people could try at home,” Palau adds.
While Palau has had a hand in the style’s revival, the French-twist boom is a part of a larger pendulum swing from sweatpants, messy buns, and frictionless athleisure to glamour and a little effort. “Fashion is moving back toward refinement and intention, and the French twist delivers that instantly,” says renowned hairstylistJawara Wauchope, whose clients include Rihanna and the Victoria’s Secret Fashion Show. “The resurgence really comes from its versatility and restraint. You can take hair in almost any state—messy, wet, or meticulously clean—and pin it up in a way that always feels chic. It’s elegant and strong, effortless but disciplined. I think people are craving beauty that feels composed and minimal, yet with real presence.”
Twisted updos have been around since ancient Greek and Roman times. Ivory and wooden combs were even found in Egyptian tombs, used not only for grooming and securing hairstyles but also to signify wealth and status. Marie Antoinette may have popularized the elaborate pouf, but it was Audrey Hepburn’s perfectly coiffed French twist in 1961’sBreakfast at Tiffany’sthat made the style so enduring. The image of that teased updo, fixed with a tiny combed tiara, still reigns across thousands of Pinterest boards as the ultimate inspiration. A version of the twist was even spotted on the red carpet at this year’s Golden Globe Awards, where Zoë Kravitz and hairstylist Nikki Nelms adorned their take on a twist with a diamond flower pin at the crown and loose, face-framing tendrils.
An updo without an accessory is like a house with no foundation: It would fall apart.Gossip Girland get-ready-with-me videos ushered in the era of the oversize headband; claw clips have come back into popularity, along with everything else resurrected from the ’90s. The comb-studded French twist is the natural evolution of our obsession with hair ornamentation.
Only this version is less precious. If the original ’60s iteration evoked a lifestyle where women had the proclivity (and time) to reach for coiffed, shellacked, no-strand-out-of-place precision, today’s French twist is meant to fit the times, so it’s okay if it’s a little imperfect or, for lack of a better phrase, effed up. “We’re allowing texture, movement, and individuality to live within the shape,” Wauchope says. “The modern twist feels softer, more human, and more inclusive of different hair types and textures.”
Fashion stylistKate Young, who works with Dakota Johnson and Scarlett Johansson, has been French-twisting her hair for decades. Years of slicked-back styles have led to bun fatigue; a twist offers the same benefits, but “it feels cooler than a chignon right now,” she says. Young recently wore the style to two fancy events, noting that “it feels very uptown and elegant.” She secures her twists with combs and bobby pins, then adorns them with brooches, earrings, or even a giant vertically placed grosgrain bow, pulling inspiration from Matthieu Blazy’sChanel Métiers d’art 2026 showin New York. For her, though, it’s not just a nighttime look: “I often wear a messy one in the day with one big comb holding it up.”
This is the crux of the appeal of the modern French twist: There is no “perfect” way to do it. “It’s most important to have fun with the look,” says hairstylistBobby Eliot, who works with Gracie Abrams and Miley Cyrus. “It’s all about making it lived-in. Flyaway and wispy hairs are your friend, because that’s what will make it look cool and modern.” Pulling out hair from a polished French twist may feel counterproductive, but Palau echoes the importance of undone front pieces to create texture.
If you’re a French-twist novice, Palau suggests using bobby pins to shape the twist and even to secure the hair accessory. Don’t worry about disguising the pins either. “There was a time that you needed to hide the bobby pin, but now bobby pins are an accessory too—and something else that’s nostalgic,” adds Palau, who gave a master class in the style at theKhaite Spring 2026 runway show.While shoulder-length hair or longer is ideal, it’s not impossible to twist and pin shorter hair. (Even bobs can get in on the action.)
For a long-lasting twist, use a dry shampoo or texturizing spray, like one from Oribe, to instantly add grit. Or opt for a styling cream or balm, like Crown Affair the Finishing Gel, to avoid hair that’s “too clean and slippery,” Wauchope says. It’s a style that’s particularly great on second- or third-day unwashed hair. If you have gone too gritty, soften hair with an oil like Hair Rituel by Sisley Precious Hair Care Oil. “The goal is movement, not stiffness,” Wauchope adds. “The hair should still feel alive.”
While Palau’s creation for the Row requires an extra set of helping hands (or maybe a degree in architecture), tutorials can be helpful to learn the motions of a basic twist. Once you’ve mastered the flick of the wrist required for a French twist, there’s a certain confidence that comes from tossing your hair up and off your shoulders in a style women have been wearing for generations. It’s why a French twist looks just as right at a gala as it does at the gym.

trend_name: Six Ways You Can Actually Prevent Gray Hair, According to Experts
description:
Every product on this page was chosen by a Harper's BAZAAR editor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
Tips to at least slow the graying process way down
Aside fromhair thinning, one of the most common hair concerns that comes along with age is going gray. And while we're all about a natural hair color transition, we also understand the urge to preserve your color as long as possible—especially if it's happening before you're mentally prepared.
“Graying is simply the melanin loss of hair. Unfortunately, the loss of melanin in hair isn't just visual; it affects the way hair strands behave," explains Tiffany Nicole, hairstylist, trichologist, and founder ofEveryday Hair. (It's true:Gray hair requires special care.)
But what if we told you there may be ways to prolong that transition? Keep reading below to find out some of the key causes of graying, six ways to maintain your color as long as possible, according to experts.
It’s long been believed that genetics lead to graying—and although our parents’ and grandparents’ heads of hair are indeed a factor, they’re just a piece of the puzzle. “While genetics and the natural decline in melanin production with aging play a big role, chronic scalp inflammation, oxidative stress, and nutrient deficiency speed up the rate of graying,” says Nicole.
“In actuality, there's only one gene that is linked to causing gray hair,” explains Jay Small, a certified trichologist and co-founder of longevity haircare brandArey. “And so a lot of times the relationship between how your mother, father, or family has gray is more likely consistent with the lifestyle habits that have been passed down from them that you share with them beyond this genetic link.”
In other words, there are things you can do to alter the pattern, and some things are out of our control: “It could be as simple as the sun. It could be as deep as drinking, smoking, or eating processed foods. And then from there, there is general aging,” Small says.
Rest assured, there’s no right or wrong time to go gray. “Research has even found that textured hair starts graying usually in the 40s and 50s, while straighter hair textures usually begin graying in the 30s and 40s,” Nicole explains. “However, premature graying before the age of 30 is often a sign of oxidative imbalance.”
According to Small, graying is "inevitable." As for reversing grays, hair that's turned completely white is too far gone. Our body uses an enzyme called tyrosinase to create the pigment in our hair, and when that enzyme decreases, so too does pigment production. “There's nothing about this that we want people to interpret that we can reverse,” says Small. “It's the same way that if I went into a store and picked up a cream for wrinkles. If I put them on my forehead, I'm still going to have wrinkles.”
As for gray hair that still retains some pigment, the process can be slowed down. “There are things that you can do to preserve your pigment and to slow the process of graying,” he says, stressing that “It's really important to understand that graying is not just black or brown or reddish to white. It's a whole de-pigmenting process.”
Ahead, find out some of the ways to preserve your color longer, according to the experts.
According to the experts, increasing the amount of vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants in what you eat can make a big difference. “It's important to prioritize a diet high in vitamin B12, folate, copper, and zinc,” says Nicole.
Small looks at the diet more broadly first: “In consultation, I ask people what they’re eating and how often they’re eating it,” says Small. “Because processed foods are going to get in the way [of pigment development]. It's going to cause intrinsic oxidative stress. So removing that is going to be a big step.”
Then, he looks at specific foods more closely: “That is the protein sources, leafy greens, eggs, avocados, things like that,” he says. “But what we've learned is when you compare the amount that your body will need to show a result in your hair, it's quite a lot of food.” Therefore, you might need a little bit of help, which leads us to…
Simply put, your body isn’t prioritizing your hair when it receives its daily allotment of vitamins. “Your hair's not essential to your body. It's a ‘nice to have,’ It's not a need to have,” explains Small. “And so your diet is going to be used for the whole body.” That’s when a supplement comes into play. “But it is best when used with a good diet, and a bit of movement and exercise,” stresses Small.
So what should you add, specifically? “What we found is that copper is one of the primary sources of this melanin production.” Copper is one of the main ingredients inArey’s Not Today, Grey: “On top of that, you are going to get the complex of B vitamins, iron, copper, selenium, and an antioxidant profile that we know helps specifically in the hair follicle with pigment production.”
In a clinical study, 88 percent of users had less gray hair growth after using Arey's hero supplement for six months.
Carry over your sun protection practices to your hair—both by using scalp sunscreen and wearing hats when possible. “UV is actually one of the largest culprits of why we see changes both in our skin and our hair,” explains Small, who says the area “is really taking the brunt of that over time,” which is why the front hairline and part tend to go gray before other areas.
Non-greasy and water-resistant for up to 80 minutes, this sunscreen mist protects both the hair and scalp.
If you’ve been using the same shampoo and conditioner for years, it might be time for a change. Consider treating your haircare like skincare, with products focused on the issue(s) you’re looking to address—i.e., graying. “You can prevent the yellowing of your hair and encourage a healthy transition to gray hair with a scalp-first approach that supports your follicle health and hair fibers,” says Nicole. “Adjust your hair regimen to include gentle cleansers, anti-inflammatory ingredients, moisturizing products, and UV filters.”
This sulfate-free formula is packed with antioxidants to protect hair and boost color vibrancy.
It sounds counterintuitive, but dyeing your hair at the first sign of grey could actually lead to more graying. “A lot of people use hair dye too early, and then you're putting this stress on the scalp to kind of get rid of these grays, and then all of a sudden that makes more and more grays,” explains Small.
Instead, he says to view the gray hair as a sign. “What I want people to see is, oh, my body's telling me a message,” he says. If the gray bothers you, look into your diet and possible deficiencies—then go from there.
Drinking, smoking, not sleeping—all of the above can cause stress in the body, which can (spoiler) affect your hair. “If you are someone who has a lifestyle where you're outside a lot, you're not eating super healthy, you're smoking cigarettes, you're drinking, you're not sleeping, you're going to be in the camp of premature graying because of all those factors,” explains Small.
That said, the methodology isn’t foolproof—and you could still experience premature graying even if you do all the “right” things. “We see this time and time again, but the majority of people will preserve their hair color longer if they aren't doing these things that have this kind of internal and external effect,” says Small.

trend_name: Shop Carolyn Bessette Kennedy's Favorite '90s Headbands for Under $40
description:
Every product on this page was chosen by a Harper's BAZAAR editor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
Here's where to find the trendsetter's go-to hair accessory, as seen inLove Story
Few people serve as such a timeless source of fashion inspiration as Carolyn Bessette Kennedy. The ‘90s style icon is in the cultural conversation more than ever, with Ryan Murphy's upcoming seriesLove Storydetailing her romance with the late John F. Kennedy Jr., which premieres on February 12. The former Calvin Klein publicist is best known for her minimalist, sleek style—and her penchant for classic acetate headbands is no exception.
The tortoiseshell headband became a major part of CBK’s signature '90s style, worn to adorn a sleek ponytail or to keep her long blond hair out of her face (usually accompanied by small oval sunglasses). If you’re looking to channel her look ahead of Murphy’s biopic, then you’re in luck, because New York apothecary C.O. Bigelow has the exact same headbands in stock in a handful of tortoise patterns—all under $40.
The iconic apothecary in Greenwich Village has been in business since 1838, and it’s rumored that CBK was a frequent shopper, with tortoiseshell headbands likely in her haul. The French-made headbands from Charles Wahba are made of high-shine acetate and stay-put teeth to keep hair perfectly taut. The headbands are made exclusively for C.O. Bigelow.
Here's a little tip from our beauty editors: while the 1.5-inch headband isexcellentat keeping hair in place, the tight, tooth-comb interior can feel uncomfortable and even painful on the head when worn for hours (trust us—we've tried them). So maybe skip this look if you have a sensitive scalp or are prone to headaches.
If you're looking to get your hands on a CBK-inspired headband quickly before theLove Storypremiere, below are some other fast-shipping options that look just as sophisticated.

trend_name: What’s More On-Trend, the Side Part or Center Part?
description: The center part has aslightadvantage in the trend department “for at least the rest of the year,” according to New York City hairstylistJackson Simmonds. That being said, you really can’t go wrong with either look. “I am definitely starting to see the side part make its way back,” says Puncerelli. “Let me be clear: The side part has never been dead. She just took a nap.”

trend_name: I Heard Millennials Wear Side Parts, and Gen Z Wears Middle Parts. Is That True?
description:
Sort of.“I do believe there is a generational divide between Millennials and Gen Z about the right way to part your hair,” says Puncerelli. “I think somewhere along the way, the side part was demonized by Gen Z, claiming the middle part as the ‘fresh, clean girl’ aesthetic." (Of course, this look is just a reinterpretation of styles that have been around since the '90s, too.)
Simmonds says the part debate was “overblown and hyped up on social media.” In real life, he says, “I see looks going both directions.” Still, he admits, “It's mostly been Gen Z to have had the center part in a chokehold.”
Both stylists agree that personal taste and style also play a big factor, along with age group. “In reality, it all comes down to personal preference,” says Puncerelli. She points to Gen Z influencers like Alix Earle, who often wears a side part, as someone bucking the "trend."

trend_name: Is the Side Part Back in Style?
description: Unequivocally, yes. “Fashion is cyclical, and like it or not, the side part is making its way back,” says Simmonds. He says the look is “Not a hard transition," but rather “about adding to your roster of looks by simply making the flip.”

trend_name: What Part Looks Better for My Face Shape?
description:
Play around with your hair part and see what you like best. According to Simmonds, a middle part “elongates and flatters balanced face shapes, while side parts suit round and square faces, and suit curls beautifully.”
You can also change it up according to your mood. “I like to part my hair both ways, and, in general, wearing a middle part or a side part is flattering on most face shapes,” says Puncerelli. “A middle part definitely gives off a little bit more modernity, and a side part is more classic.” What you’re wearing plays a role, too. “Some looks are better with a middle part, some look sharper when your hair is parted to the side,” she says.

trend_name: What If I Have a Cowlick?
description:
If you have a stubborn section of hair keeping you from wearing your part one way or another, you’re not alone. “A cowlick is a tuft of hair that grows in a different direction than the rest, forming a swirl that often causes the hair to stick up a bit more,” explains Puncerelli. “The way to tame a cowlick is by styling it in the direction of the cowlick's natural growth pattern and using a styling product to hold the hair in place.”
But there are ways to deal with them. “Once I am completely finished styling my hair, I like to over-direct the blow dryer on top of my cowlick—especially the one at the hairline—and hit it with cool air to set it so it lies more flat,” she advises.
When in doubt, have a few great styling products on hand to help, no matter what part you choose.

trend_name: Inside the Grand Opening of the L'Oréal Academy in New York City
description:
The company’s new education center is designed to level up the expertise of professional hairstylists and colorists.
There's a new hub for expert-level hair education in New York City. Earlier this month, L'Oréal Professional Products opened theL'Oréal Academy, drawing pro hairstylists, brand ambassadors, and celebrities and content creators from around the country to celebrate.
After a signature hair-cutting ceremony and cocktail hour, attendees enjoyed an energetic presentation complete with live model hairstyling, spoken word, and choreography, a convergence of hair artistry, dance, fashion, and music.
Like its sister academy in El Segundo, California, the East-Coast outpost serves as a training ground for licensed hair professionals in color, technique, and business across L'Oréal Professional Products brands—Redken, Matrix, Mizani, Color Wow, Kérastase, Pureology, L’Oréal Professionnel Paris, Biolage Professional Hair Spa, Shu Uemura art of hair, and Pulp Riot.
L'Oréal Professional Products invests in the future of the beauty industry by employing more than  700 hair experts nationwide and providing them with a robust curriculum for a well-rounded expertise. "It all starts with the best educators," says Leslie Marino, president of L'Oréal US Professional Products. "When you took a class in school, the curriculum mattered, but what mattered more was the teacher. What differentiates us is the quality and reach of our hairdressers across multiple disciplines: color, cut, finish, textured hair—you name it. That's what makes us special and different." Marino adds that teaching professionals to master textured hair and all curl types is a top priority for L'Oréal. "It's a necessity, it's not a nice-to-have," she says.
Students attending the academy will have first-hand access to the latest product innovations from the brands, many of which have not even been released to the public. The Academy also contains an on-site testing center, anchoring professionals in each step of new developments, as well as furthering an overall education. The new academy furthers L’Oréal's greater mission to elevate the hair industry by bringing salon professional beauty and services to all.

trend_name: The Expert Guide to Caring for Gray Hair
description:
Every product on this page was chosen by a Harper's BAZAAR editor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
A trichologist and celebrity hairstylist weigh in
Embracinggray hairis a hair decision that transcends trends—after all, grays happen to all of us—but thanks tocelebrities like Andie McDowell and Salma Hayek, going silver has never been more of-the-moment. And the good news is, there are several steps you can take to give your gray all the sheen and strength it needs along the way. Because gray hair isn't just regular hair that's turned a different color; it's different from the inside out.
"Gray hair is drier, coarser, and more sun-sensitive than pigmented hair, so it needs extra hydration, heat protection, and regular trims to stay soft, smooth, and polished," explains celebrity hairstylist and gray color expertMia Santiago.
Ahead, find out some of the steps you can take to take care of gray hair, so it looks and feels its best, according to Santiago and Jay Small, a certified trichologist and co-founder of longevity haircare brandArey.
If nourishing your hair was important before, it’s even more important as we age. “Gray hair needs that because there are textural changes that are inherently associated with the lack of pigment and the lack of the disulfide bonds,” explains Small, referring to the chemical bonds that maintain the structure of hair’s keratin protein.
Small says he views gray hair like a balloon that’s starting to deflate. “I want to fill it up with moisture and nourishing ingredients,” he explains.
Luckily, there are plenty of products that can help in this area: “Rich conditioners, weekly masks, and a good leave-in are key,” says Santiago. Her favorite?AGE beautiful Hydrating Mask, which "rehydrates and moisturizes wiry, coarse silver hair."
“I think many of us have adopted a more robust skincare routine as we've gotten older, and then shampoo and conditioner kind of remains the same,” says Small. He stresses the importance of a basic knowledge of ingredients and formulas. “If you have gray hair that's coarse or dry or brittle, if something's too cleansing, too stripping, that can be a very easy place to start,” he says. “There are now a lot of ingredients within shampoo that we can look for with antioxidants andpeptides that will nourish the scalpas well.”
On that same token, there are certain ingredients to avoid, like sulfates, which act as a surfactant, the agent that causes shampoo to lather up. According to Small, the best shampoo for gray hair may not be quite as sudsy as the ones you're used to—and that’s OK. “They’re potentially not the best thing for hair and for our scalps, and that might mean less suds,” he says.
For conditioners, he recommends going silicone-free. “There are a lot of great natural oils, extracts and alternatives. Sunflower oil, jojoba oil, and shea butter are great materials that will absorb into a hair strand and won't coat the hair strand like silicones do.” He adds that while “there are some good versions” of silicones, “the majority of them sit on the surface, they weigh the hair down, and they make hair feel greasy.”
“The scalp gets drier with age, so gentle exfoliation, lightweight hydrating serums, and a little massage help with circulation and follicle health,” says Santiago.
Small also recommends gentle exfoliation, noting that anything too harsh could affect the scalp’s microbiome. “We make one that is a salicylic, glycolic, lactic acid mixture, but without the abrasion,” he says of the Scrub by Arey.
Both experts stress the importance of a good scalp massage to stimulate circulation. “Scalp massages are really important, whether it's with your fingertips or a dry brush,” says Small. That being said, he says to be extra careful—especially when the scalp is wet—to avoid excess tugging or pulling.
Also, use a scalp serum for bonus benefits, like Dr. Barbara Sturm Super Anti-Aging Scalp Serum, which has scalp- and hair-strengthening ingredients like hyaluronic acid and vitamin B5.
Cutting your hair is one of the best ways to ensure hair health, no matter your color. Plus, it just helps gray hair look polished. “Regular trims and gentle styling can help prevent frizz and maintain an intentional look,” says Santiago.
UV rays also affect your hair, and of course, your scalp. “Don’t forget sunscreen on your part,” reminds Santiago. “Gray hair reflects more UV, and the scalp burns faster.” For extra credit, wear a hat when you're in direct sunlight.
Gray hair tends to have a different texture and finish than other types of hair, so don’t be surprised if your styling routine needs a rethink, too. And that might mean more heat-styling (with proper prep and heat protection, of course): “If you used to wear your hair natural and let it dry naturally, you might need to polish it a little bit more,” says Small.
Gray color can look dull, too—and that’s where a purple shampoo can work wonders to brighten it up. Santiago stresses to “Protect hair from the sun, use neutralizing shampoos, and add a clear or silver gloss for shine” for bright gray or white color, recommending Clairol Shimmer Lights to get the job done.
If graying or thinning happens suddenly, it might be time to check in with your doctor in addition to your trusted hairstylist. “What graying indicates is that there's a change happening in the body,” says Small. “Typically, we look at it as a barometer—whether it's nutrition, active stress, general age, stress in general, sleeping, et cetera.”
Santiago concurs, adding that “managing stress, supporting your nutrition, and avoiding chronic heat or harsh chemicals can slow the process and keep your hair healthy longer.”

trend_name: Four Easy Ways to Refresh Your Bangs, According to Expert Hairstylists
description:
Ahead, your ultimate guide to maintaining bangs—no salon visit needed
Every product on this page was chosen by a Harper's BAZAAR editor. We may earn commission on some of the items you choose to buy.
Next to chopping your hair into abobor even apixie cut, bangs are a surefire way to transform and elevate your look.  Fromcurtain bangsto wispy face-framing pieces, there's a style that can suit anyone and any aesthetic. However, bangs, also known as fringe, look chic and structured after a fresh cut, but they can be tricky to maintain.
While bangs are transformative, they require upkeep. That doesn't mean you need to go to the salon weekly for trims. Keeping bangs in shape can even mean spending a few extra seconds combing fringe pieces into place. Maintaining the shape isn't the only important part of having bangs. Since bangs make up a tiny portion of your overall hair, it can be easy to overlook caring for them—especially since the secret to keeping them healthy and looking fresh is caring for the strands.
Ahead, hairstylists and experts share the best ways to maintain your bangs.
If you recently cut bangs, you'll likely frequent the hair salon more often than you did before. In general, hairstylists recommend a trim every three to four weeks, depending on how quickly your hair grows. But with fringe, you may need more frequent trims. "I tend to advise clients to have one or two fringe trims between haircut appointments. Usually, you will be able to tell when it gets to the point where it’s harder to style," says Mark Sherwood, senior stylist and color specialist atBrooks and Brooks salon. If your hair grows fast, these in-between trims will help maintain the shape.
If you can't get to the salon, don't fret. With expert styling, you can make your overgrown fringe look fresh. "Lightly blend the edges with a round brush and blow-dryer, curling or bending it more than you normally would, to compensate for the overgrowth," suggests Eugene Smith, senior stylist atJohn Frieda salon.
Because fringes often sit against the forehead, they tend to get greasy rather quickly. Unless you wash your hair daily, having a dry shampoo in your arsenal can be useful to absorb excess oil. It also gives bangs extra texture, lift, and body.
If your bangs need restyling but you can't wash your whole head, take a spray bottle of water, lightly mist the bangs, and hit it with a blow-dryer to re-style. "Try not to touch them too much. The natural oils from your hands can make it separate or go limp," adds Smith.
When getting ready, make sure to always pin your fringe with creaseless clips so it doesn't come into contact with any oils or emollient products from your makeup and skincare routine.
Given that it's quicker to blow-dry and style bangs than to style your full head, it's easy to forget to use a heat protectant. But fringe bangs are still susceptible to heat damage from hot tools. To take extra care when styling, use a tiny amount of a heat protectant—whether a blow-dry spray or a protecting cream—to avoid overloading the strands.
"Remember, less is more," Smith says. "Cut tiny amounts at a time. You can always take more off, but you can’t put it back." When cutting, Smith says to start with dry hair, since wet hair can shrink as it dries, making it easy to cut damp hair shorter than planned. Don't use kitchen shears or a regular pair of scissors—opt forhairdressing scissors, which have sharper, more precise blades for cutting hair. Lastly, twist the fringe and trim very lightly. Take small, vertical snips into the fringe using the point of the scissors, also known as point cutting, rather than cutting straight across.
